issueTitle: Updating the nightly toolchain for all LTS branches?

issue.title: Updating the nightly toolchain for all LTS branches?
issue.author: tcharding
issue.description:
We are pushing to use `cargo rbmt` for all LTS release branches. Perhaps after that we should patch the update-nightly-toolchain bot to raise a PR against each of the LTS branches? 

Requires: #6163 (and similar to `0.31.x`).
requirements: should patch the update-nightly-toolchain bot to raise a PR against each of the LTS branches
discussionHighlights: If anyone picks this up please do not delete `./nightly-toolchain` until explicit ack from @apoelstra to do so.

You can go ahead and delete it, your PR just won't be merged for a couple days at least :).

concept ACK running the bot on the LTS branches.

risks.impact: Harder to verify correctness and prevent regressions

steps.title: Setup and Preparation
actions: Create a new feature branch from main/master.
Read the issue title, requirements, and acceptance criteria until you can restate the objective as: "should patch the update-nightly-toolchain bot to raise a PR against each of the LTS branches".
Open the first likely implementation files before coding: 0.31.x.
Trace where those files are imported or invoked so you understand the full code path that will change.
Run the project or test baseline once so you know the current behavior before introducing changes.
pitfalls: Do not start patching the first file you see without tracing where the relevant behavior actually enters the system.

resources.title: Create Branch
resources.description: Suggested branch name based on the issue title.

resources.title: README Context
resources.content:
Rust Bitcoin

Library with support for de/serialization, parsing and executing on data-structures and network messages related to Bitcoin.

[Documentation](https://docs.rs/bitcoin/) Supports (or should support) * De/serialization of Bitcoin protocol network messages * De/serialization of blocks and transactions * Script de/serialization * Private keys and address creation, de/serialization and validation (including full BIP-0032 support) * PSBT v0 de/serialization and all but the Input Finalizer role. Use [rust-miniscript](https://docs.rs/miniscript/latest/miniscript/psbt/index.html) to finalize.
resources.description: Read this first so you understand the project purpose and contributor workflow.

actions: Open `0.31.x` and identify the exported function, class, or handler that currently owns the behavior closest to: should patch the update-nightly-toolchain bot to raise a PR against each of the LTS branches.
Trace the inputs, outputs, and adjacent callers around `0.31.x` so you know exactly where the new behavior must be inserted.
Adjust the existing logic in `0.31.x` to improve the current behavior without regressing supported flows.
Add or update nearby type guards, return types, or schema handling in `0.31.x` if the new behavior changes data shape.
Keep the implementation aligned with existing patterns already used in neighboring files to avoid introducing a one-off design.
pitfalls: Do not add parallel logic paths in `0.31.x` if the existing code already has a single place that owns this behavior.

resources.content: Bob treats `0.31.x` as investigation context, not a confirmed code-edit location. Prefer executable source files before generating a patch preview in remote mode.

steps.title: Add New Tests
actions: Create or update focused tests near the implementation area: the nearest existing test file.
Write one happy-path test that proves the issue objective now works: should patch the update-nightly-toolchain bot to raise a PR against each of the LTS branches.
Add at least one edge-case or failure-path test that would have caught the regression or missing behavior earlier.
If the issue changes interactions between modules, add one integration-style assertion that proves the wiring still works end-to-end.
pitfalls: Do not create tests that mirror the implementation too closely; assert observable behavior instead.
steps.description: Create comprehensive tests for new functionality
validationCriteria: The tests prove the main expected outcome for: should patch the update-nightly-toolchain bot to raise a PR against each of the LTS branches.
At least one edge case or failure path is covered.
The new tests fail without the implementation change and pass with it.

steps.title: Update Documentation
actions: Update inline comments only where the changed behavior in 0.31.x would otherwise be hard to understand.
If "should patch the update-nightly-toolchain bot to raise a PR against each of the LTS branches" changes developer-facing or user-facing behavior, document that change in README or nearby docs.
If the change is internal-only, say so explicitly and keep the documentation local to the code instead of editing the README.
Do not add broad documentation churn; document only the new contract, caveat, or usage pattern introduced by this issue.
pitfalls: Do not add broad README churn if the issue only changes an internal behavior contract.

resources.title: Current Code: README.md
resources.content: [Documentation](https://docs.rs/bitcoin/) Supports (or should support) * De/serialization of Bitcoin protocol network messages * De/serialization of blocks and transactions * Script de/serialization * Private keys and address creation, de/serialization and validation (including full BIP-0032 support) * PSBT v0 de/serialization and all but the Input Finalizer role. Use [rust-miniscript](https://docs.rs/miniscript/latest/miniscript/psbt/index.html) to finalize. For JSONRPC interaction with Bitcoin Core, it is recommended to use [rust-bitcoincore-rpc](https://github.com/rust-bitcoin/rust-bitcoincore-rpc). It is recommended to always use [cargo-crev](https://github.com/crev-dev/cargo-crev) to verify the trustworthiness of each of your dependencies, including this one. ## Known limitations ### Consensus This library **must not** be used for consensus code (i.e. fully validating blockchain data). It technically supports doing this, but doing so is very ill-advised because there are many deviations, known and unknown, between this library and the Bitcoin Core reference implementation. In a consensus based cryptocurrency, such as Bitcoin, it is critical that all parties are using the same rules to validate data, and this library is simply unable to implement the same rules as Core. Given the complexity of both C++ and Rust, it is unlikely that this will ever be fixed, and there

projectActionGuide.title: Orient Yourself
actions: Start with the GitHub README, repo description, and issue discussion to confirm the project’s purpose and contributor expectations.
Open the likely entry points first: bitcoin/embedded/src/main.rs, hashes/embedded/src/main.rs.
Map the top-level working areas before coding: bitcoin, fuzz, fuzz/fuzz_targets.

projectActionGuide.title: Run the Project Correctly
actions: Install and validate the baseline with `npm install` and the project’s test script if available.

projectActionGuide.title: Trace the Code Path
actions: Trace the request or event flow from handlers into service modules and shared utilities before patching logic.
Follow existing naming, directory, and test placement conventions instead of introducing a new layout for this issue.

projectActionGuide.title: Scope the Issue Change
actions: Translate "Updating the nightly toolchain for all LTS branches?" into one small code path to change first, then expand only if the tests and issue discussion require it.
Update the nearest existing tests or create a focused regression test next to the implementation area rather than adding broad new coverage first.
Keep a short notes file or PR draft listing assumptions, repo-specific constraints, and any follow-up tasks that should not be bundled into v1.

Cross-check your implementation against the issue discussion, especially this guidance: If anyone picks this up please do not delete `./nightly-toolchain` until explicit ack from @apoelstra to do so.
